items.q: What was the Elizabethan Religious Settlement of 1559?
items.correct: A deliberate via media — a Protestant doctrine and English-language prayer book combined with some Catholic-style ceremony — designed to maximise conformity.
items.misconception: Elizabeth made England fully Protestant and banned Catholic worship outright.
items.random_wrong: Elizabeth reunited England with the Pope to make peace with Spain.
items.why: The settlement was a compromise designed to be broadly acceptable. Students assume binary Protestant/Catholic divides; Elizabeth deliberately blurred them to keep her realm stable.

items.q: Why was the Spanish Armada defeated in 1588?
items.correct: A combination of English tactics and fireships, poor Spanish planning, and the 'Protestant wind' storms that wrecked the fleet on the return.
items.misconception: Drake defeated the Armada single-handedly in a great sea battle.
items.random_wrong: Elizabeth I personally commanded the English fleet at sea.
items.why: Drake is mythologised. The English fleet harassed but did not destroy the Armada in battle — most Spanish ships were lost to storms around Scotland and Ireland.

items.q: Why was Vesalius significant in the history of medicine?
items.correct: His 1543 De Humani Corporis Fabrica corrected Galen's errors via human dissection and accurate illustration, founding modern anatomy though not curing disease.
items.misconception: Vesalius discovered the cause of disease and ended humoral theory.
items.why: Vesalius improved anatomy but disease theory still ran on humours for centuries. Students conflate progress in one area (structure) with progress in another (cause of illness).

items.q: In what year did Elizabeth I become queen?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 1558. “1547” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Who served as Elizabeth's chief minister for around 40 years?
items.why: It's William Cecil. “Walsingham” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Roughly how many advisors sat on Elizabeth's Privy Council?
items.why: Exact answer: 19. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
